text: The surgeon performs an initial survey of the abdominal cavity, checking for extra colonic disease or metastasis. The sigmoid mesentery is lifted to identify the inferior mesenteric artery pedicle. Dissection begins just below the arterial pedicle, carefully avoiding the pre-sacral vessels and nerves. The avascular plane is visible, allowing for safe dissection using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon identifies the ureters, which are gently dissected and pushed posteriorly. The dissection plane is developed from medial to lateral, incising the lateral pelvic attachments of the sigmoid colon and upper rectum along the line of Toldt. The fascia of Toldt is left intact, and dissection is carried close to the mesentery. Care is taken to preserve the gonadal vessels, ureter, and greater vessels such as the external iliac arteries and veins. The posterior plane is opened out after dissection from medial to lateral. Attention is then turned towards isolating the inferior mesenteric pedicle.

The surgeon retracts the pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery, leaving a 1.5 cm stump. Fibro-fatty tissues surrounding the artery are dissected, isolating the artery posteriorly. Care is taken to preserve nearby nerves. The artery is circumferentially dissected and ligated using hemolog clips, which are reinforced with metal clips before transecting the pedicle. This division is crucial for oncological clearance of lymph nodes associated with rectal and sigmoid lesions.

The surgeon clips and transects the pedicle of the inferior mesenteric artery, increasing the mobility of the descending colon for a low anastomosis. The pedicle is divided using sharp scissors, followed by lateral dissection to isolate, clip, and divide the branch of the inferior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon uses medium-large titanium clips to clip and divide the inferior mesenteric vein. The rectum is lifted anteriorly, and traction and counter-traction are applied. The lateral attachments of the peritoneum are incised, and dissection is carried out in the holy plane of Heald, between the visceral fascia and the endopelvic fascia. The nerves are pushed posteriorly inside the pre-sacral fascia, while the mesorectum, containing fat, lymph nodes, and vessels, lies between the visceral layer of the endopelvic fascia. The dissection continues down to the Valde's fissure, which is sharply incised to reach the brim of the levator ani muscle.

The surgeon uses harmonic dissection to carefully maintain the plane between the visceral and parietal layers, avoiding entry into the mesorectum to prevent bleeding. The dissection proceeds circumferentially, starting posteriorly, then laterally, and finally anteriorly. The coning of the muscular rectal tube is visible as the mid rectum is crossed, with lateral attachments incised and anterior dissection carried down below the bladder.

The surgeon enters the Denonvilliers fascia, dissecting between its layers anteriorly. Laterally, the lateral rectal stalks containing the middle rectal vessels are addressed using harmonic devices. The dissection proceeds within the loose areolar plane of the rectum. Circumferential dissection is completed, and the distal rectal stump is prepared using an Ethicon Endopath powered 60 mm green stapler, ensuring the clamp is applied below the rectum. An additional stapler load is used to ensure the rectal tube is fully transected without tissue entanglement in the staple line.

The surgeon ensures a 360-degree view of the stapler tip and shaft during firing. After firing the second load of staples, any remaining tissue bridge is cut with scissors. The distal end of the proximal colon is exposed, and a purse string suture is applied using 2-0 Prolene around the anvil of the CDH stapler. The anvil is inserted into the colon, and the purse string is tied securely. The specimen is brought inside, and the wound is closed with an ALICE stapler. Pneumoperitoneum is recreated, and the circular stapler head is brought transrectally, puncturing through the distal rectal stump. The anvil is aligned with the stapler head.

The bladder, seminal vesicles, and prostate are lifted anteriorly, ensuring bubble-to-bubble approximation without entangling other tissues. The surgeon uses a 29 mm Ethicon stapler to complete the anastomosis, cutting in a single go. The proximal purse string is freed and removed. The stapler is gently removed with upward, downward, and lateral tilts to avoid undue traction on the anastomotic suture line. Air is inflated to check for leaks, showing colon distension without air leaks in the pelvic cavity. Saline is sucked out, hemostasis is secured, and no drain is placed.
